coredata.dc:title: Peroxynitrite and nitric oxide fluorescence sensing by ethylenediamine doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Simões, Eliana F.C.
Esteves da Silva, Joaquim C.G.
Leitão, João M.M.
coredata.dc:description: The selective fluorescence sensing of nitric oxide (NO) and peroxynitrite anion (ONOO−) in the presence of the principal reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) was achieved by fluorescence quenching through the pH variation of microwave synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) prepared from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA). The highest NO and ONOO− sensitivity, with the lowest sensitivity to the principal ROS/RNS, was demonstrated respectively at pH 4 and 10. The optimum synthesis and sensing conditions of CDs were obtained by multivariate full factorial experimental design methodologies: for NO, at pH 4, 2.5g of CA and 1000μL of EDA diluted in 15mL of water exposed for 5min to a microwave radiation of 700W; for ONOO−, at pH 7, 10–0.25g of CA and 500μL of EDA diluted in 15mL of water exposed for 5min to a microwave radiation of 700W. In these optimized conditions the synthesized CDs have a quantum yield about 40%. The quantification of NO at pH 4 and of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 were done in standard and in fortified serum solutions. Also the quantification of NO and of ONOO− in standard solutions was performed in the presence respectively of ONOO− and NO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Peroxynitrite
Nitric oxide
Sensing
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A PhD grant SFRH/BD/81074/2011 to Eliana Simões from Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia (FCT, Lisbon) is acknowledged. The use of FTIR from UCQFarma, Pharmacy Faculty of the University of Coimbra and the TEM analyses from Nanomaterials and Micromanufacturing group of Centre for Mechanical Engineering of the University of Coimbra (CEMUC) are acknowledged. Appendix A

head.ce:title: Peroxynitrite and nitric oxide fluorescence sensing by ethylenediamine doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CIQ-UP, Grupo de Ciências Biológicas e Bioanalíticas, Faculdade de Farmácia da Universidade de Coimbra, Pólo das Ciências da Saúde, 3000-548 Coimbra, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CIQ-UP, Grupo de Ciências Biológicas e Bioanalíticas, Faculdade de Farmácia da Universidade de Coimbra, Pólo das Ciências da Saúde

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CIQ-UP, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Ciências da Universidade do Porto, R. Campo Alegre 687, 4169-007 Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CIQ-UP, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Ciências da Universidade do Porto

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selective fluorescence sensing of nitric oxide (NO) and peroxynitrite anion (ONOO−) in the presence of the principal reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) was achieved by fluorescence quenching through the pH variation of microwave synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) prepared from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA). The highest NO and ONOO− sensitivity, with the lowest sensitivity to the principal ROS/RNS, was demonstrated respectively at pH 4 and 10. The optimum synthesis and sensing conditions of CDs were obtained by multivariate full factorial experimental design methodologies: for NO, at pH 4, 2.5g of CA and 1000μL of EDA diluted in 15mL of water exposed for 5min to a microwave radiation of 700W; for ONOO−, at pH 7, 10–0.25g of CA and 500μL of EDA diluted in 15mL of water exposed for 5min to a microwave radiation of 700W. In these optimized conditions the synthesized CDs have a quantum yield about 40%. The quantification of NO at pH 4 and of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 were done in standard and in fortified serum solutions. Also the quantification of NO and of ONOO− in standard solutions was performed in the presence respectively of ONOO− and NO.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are luminescent carbon nanoparticles usually with dimensions less than 10nm suspending in water or in other solvents. They have attractive analytical properties such as water solubility, high quantum yield fluorescence, high biocompatibility and good photostability [1–5]. CDs are a nontoxic and biocompatible alternative to the classic semiconductors quantum dots (QDs) nanoparticles that could be synthesized by different synthesis methodologies using different carbon precursors [6].

Microwave assisted synthesis is an efficient, rapid, simple and green methodology now frequently used for CDs synthesis. Since the first microwave CDs synthesis from glucose and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG-200) [7], several other precursors had been used: glucose/poly(acrylatesodium) [8], carbohydrates/inorganic salts [9], glycerol/phosphate [9], glycerol/4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine [10], citric acid (CA)/1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) [11], CA/amines [12], phytic acid/EDA [13], poloxamer [14], triammonium citrate/soluble phosphate [15], chitosan [16] and dopamine [17]. Depending on the synthesis conditions and for the precursors used, CDs with different dimensions and quantum yields can be obtained [7–17]. CDs microwave synthesized from CA and EDA were used for bioimaging [12] and iodide detection [11] with a reported quantum yield (Φ) respectively of 30.2 and 43.8%.

Reactive oxygen (ROS) and nitrogen (RNS) species are usually implicated in several physiological and pathological processes acting as a signalling molecule, but they also could induce cell damage [18,19]. Two of the more important ROS/RNS are the nitric oxide (NO) and the peroxynitrite anion (ONOO−). Due to their role, the analytical methodologies for their detection and/or quantification, based in different analytical methodologies, are of great relevance [20,21]. Fluorescent detection of ONOO− by nanoparticles was done by quenching methods based in thiol-capped CdTe or CdTe/ZnS quantum dots (QD) [22] and in tryptophan doped CDs [23]. Fluorescent detection of NO by nanoparticles was also done by some quenching methods based in carboxymethyl chitosan CdTe [24], in chitosan CdSe [25,26], in polymethacrylate CdSe/ZnS [27] and in triethanolamine CdSe QDs [28], by a turn on method based in iron(III) dithiocarbamates CdSe/ZnS QDs [29] and by a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) ratiometric method based in phenylenediamine-containing naphthalimide functionalized CDs synthesized from CA and EDA [30].

Due to the reported high quantum yield of the CDs synthesized from CA and EDA, these precursors were chosen in order to assess the NO and ONOO− fluorescence sensing capability of the corresponding CDs. In this paper we present the optimization of the microwave synthesis of CDs from CA and EDA in order to the ONOO− and NO selective fluorescence sensing with the higher sensitivity at different pH values. This synthesis was optimized by multivariate full factorial experimental design methodologies. The synthesized CDs show a relatively high quantum yield. The sensibility to the NO and ONOO− at different pH were verified and their quantification ability was evaluated in standard solutions and in fortified serum samples.

Citric acid (CA), 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium superoxide (KO2), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and potassium hydrogen phthalate analytical grade reagents were purchased from Sigma–Aldridch. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrogen chloride (HCl), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3) and ferrous sulphate (FeSO4) analytical grade reagents were purchased from Merck, Darmstadt (Germany). Mili-Q water with resistivity of 18.2MΩcm at 25°C was used in all experiments.

The ONOO− standard solutions were prepared in a refrigerated beaker under constant stirring by mixing 100mL of NaNO2 600mM, 100mL of H2O2 600mM in HCl 0.6M and 100mL of NaOH 3.6M. The solution will turn yellow indicating the formation of ONOO−. The agitation is maintained until no O2 is formed. Saturated standard NO solutions (1.9mM) were prepared by bubbling NO gas in deoxygenated water (after bubbling argon for 15min). The superoxide (O2
−) standard solutions were prepared by rigorous weighing of KO2, the H2O2 standard solutions by rigorous measurement of the required volume in deoxygenated water. Diluted standard solutions were prepared by rigorous dilution of the initial standard solutions for ONOO− in NaOH 0.1M and for NO, O2
− and H2O2 in deoxygenated water. The hydroxyl radical (HO.) was generated in situ by the Fenton reaction by addition of a 5-fold excess of H2O2 to the EDTA–Fe(II) complex formed with different concentrations of FeSO4.

The experiments were performed at different pH values. Phthalate buffer, phosphate buffer and carbonate buffer were respectively used for the experiments at pH 4, 7 and 10. Phosphate buffer was prepared through rigorous weighting of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4, phthalate buffer pH 4 was prepared through rigorous weighting of potassium hydrogen phthalate and carbonate buffer pH 10 was prepared through rigorous weighting of Na2CO3 and NaHCO3 for the required volume of solution. With the three buffer solutions posterior addition of NaOH or HCl 0.1M were added in order to adjust the pH. Also in order to the evaluation of the pH effect in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs the pH was adjusted with NaOH or HCl 0.1M.

In order to establish the optimum conditions of CDs synthesis for the NO and ONOO− fluorescence sensing and attending to the performed experimental designs different masses of CA and volumes of EDA were diluted in 15mL of water and posterior heated in a domestic microwave at the maximum power of 700W at different heating times. Subsequently the solutions were leaving to cool and were dissolved in water. The obtained solutions were then centrifuged at 9000rpm for 10min in order to remove the suspended impurities and a volume of 30mL were set with water.

Full factorial experimental design methodologies were used in order to establish the more adequate conditions to the CDs synthesis [31–33]. The full factorial experimental designs used were implemented with the Unscrambler® software version 7.51, Camo ASA, Norway.

The absorbance and fluorescence spectra were acquired in a standard 1cm fluorescence quartz cell and collected respectively in a Jasco V-530 UV-Visible spectrophotometer and in a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorimeter. The absorption spectra were obtained in a wavelength range from 250 to 650nm with a 2nm interval, slit widths 2nm and wavelength scan rate medium; the fluorescence spectra were obtained in a wavelength range from 300 to 600nm with a 1nm interval, slit widths 5nm, sensitivity response medium, response time fast and wavelength scan rate 1000nm/min. Excitation emission matrices (EEM) were obtained in a wavelength range from 350 to 600nm for emission and a range of 220–450 for excitation with a 1nm interval; slit widths 5nm, sensitivity response medium.

Fluorescence measurements were also made using a QE65000 charge-coupled detector (CCD), a 380nm light emitting diode (LED), a sampling compartment (CUV-ALL-UV 4-way) and two 1.0mm core diameter fibre optics (P1000-2-UV–VIS) from Ocean Optics. One of the fibres guides the light from the source to the sampling compartment and the other guide the emitted light to the detector. The reaction time profiles were obtained collecting the signal at the maximum emission wavelength of 496nm every 10s with an integration time of 300ms.

The quantum yield (Φ) of the CD was evaluated comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities and the absorbance values of the CD with the ones of the quinine sulphate reference.
Φ =
Φ R
×
G r a d e
G r a d
e R
×
η 2
η R 2

In the equation Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, Grade is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance and η is the refractive index. The subscript R refers to reference fluorophore, quinine sulphate of known Φ. The literature Φ of the quinine sulphate is Φ =0.54 [34]. The η of the quinine sulphate in 0.1M H2SO4 and of CD aqueous solutions used were η =1.33.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by a Perkin Elmer FTIR Spectrum 400 in solid state with an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory in a range of 4000–650cm−1 with 16 accumulations and a resolution of 4cm−1.

The particle size and their frequency size distribution of the synthesized CDs in the optimum synthesis conditions was obtained by dynamic light scattering (DLS) in a Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern. The shape and size of these CDs were also evaluated by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) analysis in a Fei Company Tecnai G2 20 S-Twin electronic microscopic.

The detection of the principal ROS/RNS by these CDs was done at the main fluorescence band for the three levels of concentrations (10, 50 and 100μM). The results obtained shows that the presence of H2O2 and O2 .− do not provoke a significant variation in the fluorescence intensity at the three levels of concentration and only the NO and ONOO− provoke quenching of fluorescence. Higher quenching, and consequently sensitivity, is observed for ONOO− (supplementary information).

Attending to these preliminary results, the quenching effect of NO and ONOO− was assessed at different pHs at the three levels of concentrations in order to checked the possibility of the selective detection of the NO and ONOO−. The results obtained at the pHs 4, 5, 6, 7 and 10 are presented in Fig. 2 . This figure shows a greater quenching effect by the NO at pH of 4, 6 and 7 and by the ONOO− at pH 6, 7 and 10. A different response was observed at pH 4 with the selective quenching induced by the NO and, at pH 10, with the selective quenching induced by the ONOO−. Considering these results, the selective sensing with a higher sensitivity of NO (pH 4) and of ONOO− (pH 10) could be expected. Also the selective fluorescence detection of ONOO− at pH 7 could be evaluated with different synthesis conditions attending to the higher quenching effect by this RNS in comparison with NO.

Taking into consideration the previous information, an experimental optimization design was done to improve the selectivity and sensibility of the CDs towards NO and ONOO− (supplementary information). The quenching induced by NO and ONOO− at the maximum fluorescence intensity were assessed at pH 4, 7 and 10. The results show that a different m
CA is needed for the selective sensing of NO at pH 4, and of ONOO− at pHs 7 and 10: a higher m
CA for the sensing of NO and ONOO− respectively at pH 4 and 10; and, a lower m
CA for the sensing ONOO− at pH 7.

The analysis of Table 1 shows that the greater sensitivity is observed for ONOO− at pH 10 and the lowest sensitivity for NO at pH 4.
(i)
For the selective detection of NO at pH 4, and considering the sensitivity obtained (b =−2.1), linear range (2.5–100μM), greater Φ (42%) and the already high r (0.99), the selected synthesis conditions for the evaluation of the quantification capability of NO at pH 4 were m
CA
=2.5g, V
EDA
=1000μL, t
mic
=5′.
(ii)
For the selective detection of ONOO− at pH 10, and considering the greater sensitivity obtained (b =−41.6), an already great linear range (2.5–100μM), the already high r (0.99) associated to a great Φ (40%), the selected synthesis conditions for the evaluation of the quantification capability of ONOO− at pH 10 were m
CA
=0.25g, V
EDA
=500μL, t
mic
=5′. Also at pH 7 it is possible the selective fluorescence detection of the ONOO− with synthesized CDs with m
CA
=0.25g, V
EDA
=500μL, t
mic
=5′.

The fluorescence quenching effect of NO, ONOO−, H2O2, O2 .−, HO., NO2 − and NO3 − in the synthesized CDs at the selected conditions for the sensing of NO at pH 4 and of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 was assessed (Fig. 4 ). The selective response in the presence of the H2O2 and O2 .− was confirmed. Except for the HO., in the sensing of NO at pH 4, where a similar response was found for the NO and the HO., all the others results confirm the good selective response of NO at pH 4 and of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10. In comparison to the other evaluations at pH 7 and for the HO. at this pH, also a slightly higher response, similar to the obtained for the HO at pH 4, was observed. For the ONOO− at pH 7, and when compared with the response of HO., a response approximately 4× higher was obtained. The observed behaviour of HO. is due to the greatest oxidant power of the HO. However, it is important to stress, in what concern to the significance the possible interference of HO., the rather low half-live of HO. (10−9 s) when compared to the other ROS/RNS namely the NO (1–10s) and ONOO− (0.05–1s) [19]. The effect of the pH in the fluorescence intensity of the main band of CDs has no significant variation (supplementary information).

The characterization of the obtained CDs in the optimized conditions was performed. The size of the CDs in solution by DLS (supplementary information), their shape by TEM (Fig. 5 ) and the FTIR spectra of the CDs (Fig. 6 ) were obtained. DLS shows that the majority of the synthesized CDs nanoparticles have a size smaller than 10nm. Taking into consideration the main DLS peak it was found an average size of 1.0nm (m
CA
=0.25g, V
EDA
=500μL, t
mic
=5′) and of 4.8nm (m
CA
=2.5g, V
EDA
=500μL, t
mic
=5′). The TEM images confirm the size found by DLS, mostly below 10nm, and shows that a similar spherical or quasi-spherical shape was found for both synthesized CDs.

Quite different FTIR spectra were obtained for the two synthesized CDs in the optimized conditions which is due to the different CA and EDA content used in their synthesis. Beside a more complex spectrum for the synthesized CDs with a higher CA and EDA, namely at a wavenumbers lower than 1800cm−1, different wavenumbers were found for some of the principal common bands of the two spectra. The great difference was observed for the broad and strong band due to the OH and NH. For the CDs with a higher CA and EDA this band is shifted to a higher wavenumber. Small differences were found for the weak band of the CH, the sharp band of CN and one of the NH bands. Attending to the resolution of the spectra no difference was fond for the second sharp band of NH at 1546 and 1548cm−1. For the CDs with lower CA and EDA a NH band appears as a small shoulder of the other NH band. New bands are also found for the CDs with a higher CA and EDA that could be assigned to the CO of a carboxylic acid at 1780cm−1 and of an amide at 1697cm−1. Also for this CDs, in the fingerprint region (1080–1360cm−1), a great number of bands are now clearly evident that could be mainly assigned to CO and/or CN with different bond energies.

The quantification capability was evaluated for the synthesized CDs in the selected conditions for NO at pH 4 and for ONOO− at pH 7 and 10. The calibration of NO (pH 4) and ONOO− (pH 7 and 10) was done with two determinations at each calibration point used. Also attending to the linear range found, the evaluation was done in standard solutions and in fortified serum samples at three levels of concentrations (10, 25 and 50μM). Due to experimental limitations in the fortification by NO of the serum samples the quantification capability in fortified serum samples of NO was only done at the level of concentration of 10μM. Beside this assessment, the quantification capability of NO at pH 4 in the presence of ONOO− and of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 in the presence of NO were done in standard solutions at a concentration of 25μM. The results obtained are presented in Table 2 .

A highest sensitivity with a generally higher error associated to the estimative of concentration was found for the quantification of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10. Associated to the high sensitivity in the ONOO− quantification a lowest limit of detection (LOD) was found for the ONOO− at pH 7 (2.7μM) and 10 (2.0μM) in comparison to the found for the NO at pH 4 (4.6μM). The LOD found is generally higher than the obtained by other electrochemical sensors [20,21]. Nevertheless, and in comparison to the obtained by almost all of the already developed NO and ONOO− fluorescence nanoparticles optical sensors, a comparable LOD was observed [22–30]. Generally good quantifications results were found for the two RNS in the assays with the standard solutions and fortified serum samples. The lowest results were obtained for the NO at pH 4 and for the ONOO− at pH 10 at the level of concentration of 10μM respectively with the standard solution and with the standard solution and fortified serum sample. The highest results were found for the quantification of ONOO− at pH 7 in fortified serum samples at the three levels of concentrations. Similar results to the obtained with a standard solution 25μM were found in the quantification of NO and ONOO− 25μM in the presence respectively of NO and ONOO− 25μM.

The selective fluorescence sensing of NO at pH 4 and of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 by ethylenediamine doped carbon dots microwave synthesized from citric acid was established. The selective fluorescence sensing was evaluated through the assessment of the quenching by the NO and ONOO− at different pHs of the fluorescence of ethylenediamine doped carbon dots, obtained with different synthesis conditions. A greater sensitivity was found for the determination of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 and a lower sensitivity for the determination of NO at pH 4. Only for the selective fluorescence sensing NO at pH 4 a strong interference of HO. was observed although, taking into consideration the rather low lifetime of this radical, it should not constitute an interference problem. Generally good quantification results for NO at pH 4 and for ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 were obtained. Similar results were found for the quantification of NO at pH 4 and of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 in the absence and in the presence respectively of ONOO− and NO. At the levels of concentrations evaluated generally good quantification results were found with the fortified serum samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A PhD grant SFRH/BD/81074/2011 to Eliana Simões from Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia (FCT, Lisbon) is acknowledged. The use of FTIR from UCQFarma, Pharmacy Faculty of the University of Coimbra and the TEM analyses from Nanomaterials and Micromanufacturing group of Centre for Mechanical Engineering of the University of Coimbra (CEMUC) are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.06.072.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
Fluorescent carbon dots capped with PEG200 and mercaptosuccinic acid
Parallel factor analysis of EEM of the fluorescence of carbon dots nanoparticles
Carbon dots obtained using hydrothermal treatment of formaldehyde. Cell imaging in vitro
Controllable synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and their detection application as nanoprobes
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots
Microwave assisted one-step green synthesis of cell-permeable multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots without surface passivation reagents
One-step synthesis of surface passivated carbon nanodots by microwave assisted pyrolysis for enhanced multicolor photoluminescence and bioimaging
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iodide
Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis
Water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling
Rapid microwave synthesis of fluorescent hydrophobic carbon dots
A novel one-pot route for large-scale preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots powders
Microwave-assisted one-step green synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nitride dots from chitosan
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
Free radicals and antioxidants in normal physiological functions and human disease
Chemistry, physiology and pathology of free radicals
Nitro-oxidative species in vivo biosensing: challenges and advances with focus on peroxynitrite quantification
Probing the sensitive and selective luminescent detection of peroxynitrite using thiol-capped CdTe and CdTe@ZnS quantum dots
Carbon dots from tryptophan doped glucose for peroxynitrite sensing
Novel quantum dots–carboxymethyl chitosan nanocomposite nitric oxide donors capable of detecting release of nitric oxide in situ
Biocompatible quantum dots–chitosan nanocomposites for fluorescence detection of nitric oxide
NO fluorescence quantification by chitosan CdSe quantum dots nanocomposites
Quantum dot–polymethacrylate composites for the analysis of NO x by fluorescence spectroscopy
Fluorescence sensing of nitric oxide in aqueous solution by triethanolamine-modified CdSe quantum dots
Nitric oxide switches on the photoluminescence of molecularly engineered quantum dots
A fluorescent ratiometric nanosensor for detecting NO in aqueous media and imaging exogenous and endogenous NO in live cells
Response surface methodology (RSM) as a tool for optimization in analytical chemistry
Factorial analysis optimization of a Diltiazem kinetic spectrophotometric quantification method

ce:biography.ce:simple-para:
Eliana F.C. Simões finished her B.Sc. in Food Chemistry and Biochemistry and M.Sc. in Analytical Chemistry in the Chemistry Department of the University of Aveiro, Portugal. The master's degree research work was based in the development of a fibre optical sensor for monitoring frying oils degraded by frying procedures. Currently she is a PhD student in the third year of her doctoral programme at the Faculty of Sciences of the University of Porto, Portugal (FCUP). The focus of her research programme is the development of optical fluorescent nanoparticles sensors for the quantification of ROS/RNS namely the NO, ONOO−, H2O2 and O2
−.

Joaquim C.G. Esteves da Silva obtained his B.Sc., M.Sc. and Ph.D. in chemistry from the Faculty of Sciences of the University of Porto (FCUP), Portugal, where he worked on chemometric methodologies to environmental inorganic systems. Currently, he is an associate professor in the chemistry and biochemistry department of the FCUP where he teaches inorganic, environment, forensic, bioanalytical and chemometric courses. Current main research interests are the development of fluorescent nanomaterials and experimental and theoretical studies of chemiluminescent and bioluminescent systems. Also is focusing his R&D efforts on the development of biomedical, environmental and industrial innovative products.

João M.M. Leitão obtained his B.Sc. in Pharmaceutical Sciences and is Ph.D. in Pharmaceutical Chemistry at the Faculty of Pharmacy of the University of Coimbra, Portugal (FFUC). His M.Sc. in Chemical Quality Control was obtained at the Faculty of Sciences of the University of Coimbra, Portugal. His research work in order to obtain the M.Sc. and the Ph.D. was based in the development of analytical methodologies for the quantification of selected analytes respectively in environmental and pharmaceutical samples. A great focus is done in the analytical validation and in the development of the analytical methodologies with the appeal of chemometric multivariate experimental design and calibration methodologies. Currently is an assistant professor in the FFUC where he teaches Analytical Instrumental Methods of Analyses at the Integrated Master in Pharmaceutical Sciences and Bioanalytical Sciences courses. His current research work focus on the development of luminescent optical sensors namely based in fluorescent nanoparticles with the appeal of chemometric methodologies.
